coredata.dc:title: Towards efficient carbon nanodot-based electromagnetic microwave absorption via nitrogen doping
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Meng-Yuan
Lou, Qing
Zheng, Guang-Song
Shen, Cheng-Long
Zang, Jin-Hao
Liu, Kai-Kai
Dong, Lin
Shan, Chong-Xin
coredata.dc:description: The development of electromagnetic (EM) microwave absorption materials is essential for the whole electrical and electronic systems. Although carbon-based materials have been widely studied as EM absorber, the problems such as high cost, complicated processing technology and insufficient shielding performance still remain unsettled. Here, carbon nanodots (CDs) prepared through a one-step microwave-assisted heating method are demonstrated as EM absorption materials for the first time. With regulation of the doped nitrogen contents and degree of graphitization, the minimum EM reflection loss of the CDs at 10.7 GHz is −26.5 dB, and the effective absorption bandwidth of 1.5-mm thickness is 4.85 GHz. By adjusting the thickness, the effective absorption band ranges from 3.3 to 18 GHz, which is better than most non-composite carbon materials. The superior performance can be attributed to the excellent impedance matching and high dielectric loss in CDs. These results may extend the application fields of CDs and offer new insight into the exploitation of effective carbon-based EM shielding materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Microwave absorption
Nitrogen doping
Graphitization
Electromagnetic interference shielding

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CD based EM absorption materials
Electromagnetic parameters and microwave absorption properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Carbon point carbonization process (b, c) TEM (b) and HRTEM (c) images of the Raw CDs. (d) Size distribution of the Raw CDs. (e) UV–vis absorption (UV–vis) of the Raw CD and CD after heat treatment. (f) PL of the Raw CD and CD after heat treatment. The inset is the CD after heat treatment under natural light and 365 nm light. (g) FT-IR spectra of the Raw CD and CD4.

(a–c) Basic characterizations of the CDs. XRD patterns (a), Raman spectra (b) and XPS full survey spectra (c) of Raw CD (not calcined), CD1 (800 °C), CD2 (950 °C), CD3 (1100 °C), CD4 (1250 °C) and CD5 (1400 °C). (d–f) C1s (d), N1s (e), and O1s (f) spectra of CD4; (g, h) C1s (g) and N1s (h) spectra of Raw CD. (i) UV–vis absorption (UV–vis), PL excitation (Ex) and PL emission (Em) spectra of the Raw CD. The inset is the image of Raw CD under daylight condition (left) and 365 nm light illumination (right).

(a) Frequency dependence of reflection loss of the Raw CD, CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4, and CD5 at 30 wt%. (b) Frequency dependence of reflection loss for different filling amounts of CD4 with the thickness of 2.3 mm. (c) EAB of different filling amounts at 2.3 mm thickness. (d) Typical Cole-Cole semicircle of the CD4 at 50% fill.

(a–c) Illustration (a), 3D (b) and 2D (c) of reflection loss for CD4 with a filler loading of 50 wt% at different thickness. (d) Simulations of the tm versus fm under the λ/4 model and impedance matching (Z) as a function of frequency for CD4 with different thickness. (e) Comparison of electromagnetic absorbing properties of different carbon-based nanomaterials.
Schematic illustration of nitrogen-doped CDs for electromagnetic wave absorption.

head.ce:title: Towards efficient carbon nanodot-based electromagnetic microwave absorption via nitrogen doping

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Diamond Optoelectronic Materials and Devices
Ministry of Education, and School of Physics and Microelectronics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of electromagnetic (EM) microwave absorption materials is essential for the whole electrical and electronic systems. Although carbon-based materials have been widely studied as EM absorber, the problems such as high cost, complicated processing technology and insufficient shielding performance still remain unsettled. Here, carbon nanodots (CDs) prepared through a one-step microwave-assisted heating method are demonstrated as EM absorption materials for the first time. With regulation of the doped nitrogen contents and degree of graphitization, the minimum EM reflection loss of the CDs at 10.7 GHz is −26.5 dB, and the effective absorption bandwidth of 1.5-mm thickness is 4.85 GHz. By adjusting the thickness, the effective absorption band ranges from 3.3 to 18 GHz, which is better than most non-composite carbon materials. The superior performance can be attributed to the excellent impedance matching and high dielectric loss in CDs. These results may extend the application fields of CDs and offer new insight into the exploitation of effective carbon-based EM shielding materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Electromagnetic interference (EMI) and radiation are everywhere, from daily electronic equipment to space exploration and military activities. With the rapid proliferation, high integration and microminiaturization of electronic devices, electric systems and their components, a sustained increase in EMI is causing a growing concern in many aspects, such as the potential risk to human health [1–3], malfunction and deterioration of electronic equipments, and loss or interruption of communication data in the transmission process. For most of EMI shielding materials, the primary mechanism is involved in reflecting electromagnetic (EM) radiation through the charge interaction with EM fields. Despite good EMI shielding effectiveness, the mostly used EM radiation reflection materials, such as metallic materials, are often afflicted by low physicochemical stability, heavy weight and latent secondary pollution, etc. In contrast, the materials with absorption mechanism can efficiently avert such issues by turning the absorbed EM radiation into thermal energy. Nevertheless, the traditional absorption dominant shielding materials (i.e. EM absorption materials), such as metal shrouds, soft magnetic ferrites, and conducting polymers, cannot meet the absorbing requirements because of their high density, narrow absorption bandwidth, and low absorption efficiency in or above GHz region [4]. Recently, carbon-based materials with low density, good processibility, high inertness to corrosion and excellent EM absorbing performance, have attracted sustained and widespread attention [5,6]. In the family of carbon materials, graphene oxide, flexible graphite, graphene nanosheets, reduced graphene oxide, carbon microspheres, carbon nanotubes, carbon fiber and carbon black have been demonstrated for electromagnetic shielding through the route of absorption [7–18]. At present, the EM wave-absorbing performance of the existing carbon materials is far from satisfactory in minimum reflection loss (RL) (<20 dB (dB)) and effective absorption bandwidth (EAB) (<4 Gigahertz (GHz)). Although these absorption properties of carbon materials can be enhanced by compositing other magnetic materials or increasing the number of plies [19], the resulting problems such as high cost, intricate preparation process and low repeatability impedes the further application of these carbon-based composite materials. Hence, it is essential to develop new class of carbon based materials for the absorbing and shielding of EM radiation.

Carbon nanodots (CDs) as a sort of zero-dimensional carbon based nanomaterials, have shown promising applications in optoelectronics, photoelectrocatalysis, bioimaging and biosensors due to their excellent properties such as tunable band gap, high photoluminescence quantum efficiency, low cost, high physicochemical stability and good biocompatibility, etc [20–27]. With sp2 carbon skeleton and abundant functional groups, CDs can act as electron donors or acceptors, transport charge, and absorb or emit electromagnetic waves, which have been proved by their widespread applicability. So, CDs have great potential to generate similar effect in absorbing and shielding EM radiation. Overall, there are a number of advantages for CDs as the EM absorbing and shielding materials: (i) Atomically precise doping with other heteroatoms can be achieved in these CDs, which may be conducive to boosting the efficiency of EM absorbing and shielding; (ii) CDs can be prepared with controllable proportion of sp2/sp3 carbon atoms, which is suitable for enhancing impedance matching and dielectric loss; (iii) CDs have convertible surface, which is beneficial to increase their adaptability in various external environment; (iv) Low-cost CDs reveal good stability and biocompatibility, which is helpful to their practical applications. Hence, it is encouraging to develop CD-based EM absorbing and shielding materials with proper EM property. However, none such report can be found to date.

In this paper, CDs with excellent EM absorbing performance have been demonstrated for the first time. The doping amount of nitrogen atoms and ratio of sp2/sp3 carbon atoms in the CDs were further regulated by a controllable high-temperature calcination process. The effective absorption bandwidth of the single-layer CD film can reach 4.85 GHz, and its minimum reflection loss (RL) value can reach −26.5 dB. To the best of our knowledge, this is the highest EM absorbing performance reported for zero-dimension carbon based materials. The present work paves the way for the development of CDs EM absorbing and shielding materials.
Urea and citric acid come from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd, which are analytical grade without further purification.

This synthetic route is modified based on our previous work [23]. Step 1: Add citric acid (2 g) and urea (4 g) into deionized water (25 mL) and was homo-dispersed by ultrasound cleaners (10 min, 200 W). The mixed sample was placed in a commercial microwave oven with a power of 750 W and heated for about 3 min to prepare carbon dots. The prepared carbon dots were sonicated with deionized water to dissolve the carbon dots in water. The sample was then centrifugally separated to remove excess impurities to leave the carbon dot solution (5000 r/min, 10 min). And the sample was placed in a vacuum drying oven at 80 °C for 24 h. The second step: the dried carbon dots were calcined in an argon atmosphere for 2 h. The samples calcined at different temperatures were recorded as CD1 (800 °C), CD2 (950 °C), CD3 (1100 °C), CD4 (1250 °C), CD5 (1400 °C). And the carbon dots without calcination were recorded as Raw CD. The Raw CD and the reground CDs were treated at different temperatures in an argon atmosphere for 15 min, which were mixed with paraffin wax for further microwave absorption test.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-angle annular dark field (HAADF), and energy-dispersive X-ray-spectroscopy (EDX) for elemental mapping images of CDs were tested by FEI Talos F-200 TEM. X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8 Discover, Germany) were used to characterize the crystal structure of CDs under different treatment. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) was used to study the element content and chemical state of the product. A field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JSM-6700F, Japan) was used to probe the morphology of the CD samples after high-temperature treatment. The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a Hitachi UH-4150. The photoluminescence spectra of the CDs were obtained using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The Raman spectra of the CDs were determinated by LabRAM HR Evo (French). Fourier transform infrared spectra of CDs were probed by Thermo Scientific Nicolet iZ 10 spectrometer.

The electromagnetic parameters were measured by the microwave network vector analyzer (Agilent E5071C) using the coaxial method. The sample was mixed with paraffin in different mass proportions to determine the electromagnetic parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CD based EM absorption materials
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 depicts the synthesis of the nitrogen-doped CDs for EM absorption. Specifically, CDs prepared using citric acid and urea as raw materials were heat treated with a varying calcined temperature to adjust the doped nitrogen (N) contents and degree of graphitization of the CDs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images show the Raw CDs have an average diameter of around 2.2 nm (Fig. 1 b and Fig. 1d). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images demonstrate that the Raw CDs present a distinct lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) crystallographic plane of graphite, implying their high crystallinity (Fig. 1c). After the calcination at high temperature, the Raw CDs have been transformed from the independent dots into interconnected carbon layer through the self-crosslinking effect of hydrogen bonding and Van Der Waals bonding between the neighboring CDs [22]. Field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to characterize the morphology of the CDs after 1250 °C treatment, and micron-sized crystalline particles can be found from the SEM images (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S2, high angle annular dark field (HAADF) image reveals the continuous carbon layer has been established via the self-assembly of CDs after thermal treatment. Elemental mapping images from the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were also acquired to investigate the element distribution of the CDs after the calcination, showing a uniform and continuous distribution of C, N, and O in these CDs. After the calcination process, the absorption of the CDs can be extended to the whole visible and near infrared region (Fig. 1e). The strongest emission of the Raw CD aqueous solution is centered at 515 nm under 387-nm excitation. After carbonization, the strong π-π stacking effect of the graphite crystallites gradually growing in the high temperature region completely suppresses the emission of the CDs (Fig. 1f). The above structural, composition and optical characterizations reveal that high-temperature calcination is beneficial to the improvement of the graphitization degree of CDs. The change of chemical composition and surface groups of the CDs induced by calcination has been investigated by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR). The FT-IR spectrum of the Raw CDs presents the absorption peaks at 3448, 3346 and 3208 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH, OH and NH, respectively (Fig. 1g) [28,29]. The absorption peak at 1403 cm−1 belongs to the bending vibration of OH. And the peaks at 1706 cm−1 and 1619 cm−1 to CO and CN, respectively [29]. These results show that there are plenty of N, O-containing functional groups on the surface of the Raw CDs. After a high-temperature calcination at 1250 °C, the CN absorption peak intensity of the CDs at 1619 cm−1 appears a significant decline compared to that of the Raw CDs, implying the reduction of surface organic groups.

X-Rays diffraction (XRD) and Raman spectrometers have been employed to probe the structural information of the CDs (Fig. 2 a and b). These samples exhibit two broad diffraction peaks in the range of 20-30° and 40-45°, corresponding to the (002) and (101) planes of graphite carbon, respectively (Fig. 2a) [30]. After a 800 °C calcination, the full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the (002) peak reduces from 8.6° to 4.8°, indicating the increased graphitization degree of the CDs. With the increase of the calcination temperature to 950 °C, the diffraction from (101) plane becomes visible, implying that the crystallinity of the CDs has been further promoted. The Raman spectra show a typical D band at 1345 cm−1 and a G band at 1585 cm−1 (Fig. 2b). The D band is related to defects or lattice distortion in graphene, while the G band is related to in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms. The integral intensity ratio (ID /IG ) of D and G band can reflect to some extent the degree of in-plane defect concentration and crystallinity of the graphite carbon [31–33]. Tab. S1 shows that the ID /IG ratio decreases from 2.83 to 1.75 with increasing the calcination temperature, which indicates the increased degree of graphitization. This result is consistent with XRD survey.

Fig. 2c illustrates a typical XPS survey spectrum of the Raw-CDs, indicating the main constituents of the CDs are C, N, and O. The corresponding N contents in the Raw CD, CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4, and CD5 are 34.3%, 17.85%, 15%, 4.37%, 3.63%, and 1.38%, respectively. Namely, the content of N decreases gradually with the calcination temperature. The atomic ratio of C/N and C/O is in the supporting information Table. S3. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of the CD4 is shown in Fig. 2d, and the spectrum can be fitted into three Gaussian peaks centered at 284.7, 285.3, and 288.4 eV, which correspond to CC, CC, and CN/CO, respectively [29,34]. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum of the CD4 exhibits three contributions, namely pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N located at 398.1, 399.6, and 401 eV, respectively (Fig. 2e). Previous studies have demonstrated that pyridinic N and pyrrolic N mainly promote dipolar relaxation losses, while the content of graphitic N is mainly conducive to the generation of conduction loss for EM absorption [35]. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum of the CD4 is presented in Fig. 2f. The O1s spectrum have two contributions, that is CO and CO located at 532.2 and 534.2 eV, respectively. The high-resolution C1s and N1s XPS spectra of the Raw CD are shown in Fig. 2g and 2 h, which reveal similar chemical composition with that of the calcined CDs. Nevertheless, compared with Raw CD, the ratio of graphitic N in the calcinated CDs is significantly boosted, indicating that calcination process is beneficial to improve the degree of graphitization and further increase the conduction loss of EM in the CDs [36,37]. Fig. 2i shows the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the Raw CDs in aqueous solution. The maximum absorption band of the Raw CD aqueous solution is located at 410 nm, indicating an extended π–π conjugated structure [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electromagnetic parameters and microwave absorption properties
ce:section.ce:para:
The basic electromagnetic properties of EM absorbing materials can be probed from the value of complex dielectric constant (εr = ε′ − jε″) and complex permeability (μr = μ′ − jμ″). Generally, the real (ε′ and μ′) and imaginary parts (ε″ and μ″) indicate the storage and dissipation ability of electrical and magnetic energy, respectively [31,32]. The microwave absorption efficiency of a material is expressed by RL. According to the line transmission theory, the following formula can be used to calculate the RL value [6,38–39]:
(1)
RL = 20 L o
g 10
Z
in
-
Z o
Z
in
+
Z o
(2)
Z
in
=
Z o
μ r
ε r
tanh
j
2 π
c
μ r
ε r
f d
where Zin is the input impedance, Z0 is the impedance of free space, εr and μr are the complex permittivity and complex permeability, respectively. f is the frequency of the electromagnetic field. d is the thickness of the absorbing material, and c is the speed of light in vacuum [40–42].

As shown in Fig. 3 a, CD4 performs the lowest RL value in these CDs with filling ratio of 30% after high-temperature treatment, while the Raw CD does not exhibit EM absorpting properties. The detailed EM parameters of the CDs with different calcination temperature are displayed in Fig. S3. As the calcination temperature increases, the dielectric constant of the CDs increases firstly and then decreases, which corresponds to the best microwave absorption performance of the CD4. The reason for calcination temperature dependent EM absorbing property can be ascribed to the variation of N content and degree of graphitization of the CDs. As the calcination temperature increases, the proportion of graphitic N in the total N atoms of the CDs is enlarged. And the graphitization degree of the CDs has been increased. The high degree of graphitization signifies the increased conductivity and further leads to intense electrical loss in the storage and dissipation process for the EM absorption of the calcinated CDs. Meanwhile, the total N content of the CDs decreases continuously with the calcination temperature. As important heteroatoms and defects, N can serve as polarization centers to improve the dipolar polarization of the CDs, resulting in enhanced electromagnetic wave loss. Nevertheless, large N-doping content will make the conductive network sparse or have too many defects, resulting in poor electromagnetic wave dissipation performance [35]. Therefore, an appropriate N-doping concentration plays an important role in EM absorption ability of the CDs through the tradeoff of N-doping content and degree of graphitization. And the best calcination temperature is 1250 °C, which is optimal to the microwave absorption performance for the CDs.

Figs. S4a and S4b depict the real and imaginary parts of the complex dielectric constant at different filling levels, which illustrates the filling concentration-dependent electrical performance for CD4. With the adjustment of filling amount, the complex permeability of CD4 remains almost unchanged with the real and imaginary values near 1 and 0, respectively (Figs. S4d and S4e). These results mean that the loss in the storage and dissipation process of electrical energy accounts for the prime EM absorption of CDs [43]. In order to further evaluate the contribution of dielectric loss and magnetic loss to microwave absorption capacity, the dielectric loss tangent (tanδε = ε″/ε′) and magnetic loss tangent (tanδμ = μ″/μ′) have been also determinated to explore EM absorbing property of the carbon based nanomaterials. As shown in Figs. S4c and S4f, the value of tanδε of is an order of magnitude larger than that of tanδμ , indicating that the microwave absorption of CDs mainly comes from dielectric loss [44]. Simultaneously, ε′ and ε″ increases as the filling amount increases. According to the theory of free electrons, it can be deduced that the growth of ε″ is derived from the increased conductivity [44–46]. For detailed analysis, when the filling amount is changed from 20% to 30%, the real part and the imaginary parts of the dielectric constant are increased by 1.35 and 2.99 times, respectively (Fig. S4). When the filling amount is increased to more than 40%, the real and imaginary parts of the dielectric constant increase grown more than 2.85 and 26.88 times in comparison to the CDs with 20% filling amount. This phenomenon can be explained by the percolation threshold [47]. For CDs with filling ratio of 20%-30%, their dielectric constant is close to that of the paraffin matrix with no changes with the variation of frequency, which is caused by that the filling concentration is lower than the percolation threshold. When the filling rate reaches 40%, the distributional pattern of CD particles dispersed in the paraffin matrix can change from the isolated structure to the interconnected structure. The conductivity increases rapidly, leading to an increase in dielectric constant [48]. When the filling amount is above 40%, the real and imaginary parts of the dielectric constant present a decrease with increasing frequency. This frequency dispersion effect is beneficial to microwave absorption of CDs [49]. Specifically, the ε′ decreases from 13.98 to 10.2, 18.87 to 11.76, and 22.45 to 12.97 for the filling loadings of 40, 50, and 60%, respectively. Hence, the frequency dependence of reflection loss for different filling amounts of CD4 is observed (Fig. 3b). When the filling amount and thickness of CDs is kept with 20% and 2.3 mm, no contribution of microwave absorption is obtained in the region of 2–18 GHz. With the filling amount increased from 30% to 60%, the minimum RL values exhibit a variation range from −4.5 to –23 dB. Amongst the results, the CDs with filling amount of 50% show the best microwave absorption performance with a minimum RL of –23 dB due to the percolation threshold and frequency dispersion effect. As shown in Fig. 3c, when the filling ratio is 50%, the effective absorption bandwidth (EAB) reaches 2.6 GHz, which is wider than 40% and 60%.

Mostly, the dielectric loss includes polarization loss and conductivity loss. Polarization loss is divided into interface polarization, electronic polarization, dipolar polarization and ionic polarization. Electron polarization and ion polarization usually appear only when the frequency is greater than 100 GHz, so they can be excluded. In the range of 2–18 GHz, the main contribution to the dielectric loss is interface polarization and dipolar polarization [50,51]. Interface relaxation occurs in heterogeneous systems. The accumulation and non-uniform distribution of space charge at the interface will produce macroscopic electric moments, which can effectively enhance the electromagnetic wave loss. Dipole polarization occurs in molecules with significant dipole moments [52–54]. Moreover, according to the Debye theory, the relationship between ε′ and ε″ can be expressed by the following formula:
(3)
ε ′
-
ε s
+
ε ∞
2
2
+
(
ε
′ ′
)
2
=
ε s
-
ε ∞
2
2
In the equation, εs is the static permittivity, and ε∞ is the relative permittivity under high-frequency limitation. It can be seen from Formula 3 that the functional relationship of ε′ − ε″ is a semicircle, which is called Cole-Cole semicircles as a representative of Debye relaxation process [55,56]. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, a Debye relaxation process also exists in the dielectric relaxation process of CD based EM absorbing materials.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, when its thickness is 2.0 mm, its minimum RL can achieve the minimum value of −26.5 dB at 10.7 GHz with the EAB of 2.88 GHz. It is worth noting that when the thickness is reduced to 1.5 mm, its single-layer EAB exerts the maximum value of 4.85 GHz from 12.73 to 17.58 GHz. As shown in Fig. 4b and c, the CD4 with 50% filling ratio adjusted by thickness shows an effective absorption bandwidth of from 3.3 to 18 GHz, which is unmatched by other single carbon materials. Moreover, the peak frequency of the CDs is inversely proportional to the thickness of the CD filling films. With increasing the thickness of the films, the peak frequency gradually moves toward low frequencies. This phenomenon can be explained by the quarter-wave principle. Based on the quarter wavelength matching theory (λ/4), the relationship between the coating thickness (tm ) and the absorption peak frequency (fm ) can be expressed by the following formula [51]:
(4)
t m
=
n λ
4
=
nc
4
f m
ε r
μ r
( n = 1 , 3 , 5 , · · · )
Where λ is the wavelength of the microwave, c is the speed of light in vacuum, and |εr | and |μr | are the modes of εr and μr . According to the nλ/4 model, if the film thickness and absorption frequency peak satisfy the formula, the phase difference between the two reflected microwaves at the air-absorber interface and the absorber-metal interface is 180°. Then, a cancellation effect occurs, causing the two microwaves at the air-absorber interface to cancel [57,58]. In order to prove that there is interference loss in the absorber, the relationship between the absorption frequency peak fm of the sample and the coating thickness was simulated under the condition of λ/4. As shown in Fig. 4d, the red five-pointed star point represents the experimental matching thickness (texp m ) at the absorption frequency peak. The black curve represents the simulated thickness (tfit m ) using the quarter-wavelength principle in the frequency range of 2–18 GHz. It can be seen that the texp m obtained by the experiment matches the thickness (tfit m ) simulated by the quarter-wavelength principle. So, the peak frequency of the optimal RL value at each thickness can correspond to the geometric effect, indicating that the interference loss in the EM attenuation process also plays an important role. In addition, it can be seen that the excellent EM absorption capability is also the result of the excellent impedance matching characteristics and the attenuation loss. The comparison between the reported carbon-based materials and CDs is summarized in Fig. 4e and Tab. S2. It can be seen that the EM absorption performance of CD is better than other non-composite carbon materials. At the same time, it is comparable to the absorption properties of other composite materials.

In summary, EM absorption ability of N-doped CDs has been investigated for the first time. It is found that the N contents and degree of graphitization obviously affect the microwave absorption performance of the CDs through the synergistic effect of dipole polarization and impedance matching. The CDs with a calcination temperature of 1250 °C and filling ratio of 50% show an optimal RL value of −26.5 dB at 10.7 GHz and EAB of 2.88 GHz at the thickness of 2 mm. When the thickness is kept at 1.5 mm, the EAB is 4.85 GHz from 12.73 to 17.58 GHz, which surpasses the performance of most of non-composite carbon materials. The results prospect the potential of CDs as EM absorbing material, thus may find novel application field of CDs.

Meng-Yuan Wu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Qing Lou: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Guang-Song Zheng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Validation, Methodology. Cheng-Long Shen: Formal analysis, Validation. Jin-Hao Zang: Formal analysis, Validation, Methodology. Kai-Kai Liu: Writing – review & editing. Lin Dong: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Chong-Xin Shan: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of this research by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12074348, U2004168, and U1804155), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M682310), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (212300410078) and the Provincial and Ministerial Co-construction of Collaborative Innovation Center Foundation for Resource Materials (zycl202006).
